title: feat: add 'update' subcommand to self-update the CLI
description:
The CLI has no way to update itself. Users have to re-run the install script or brew upgrade manually, which creates friction and leads to version drift in the field.

Add an 'agentcarousel update' (also 'agc update') subcommand that:
- Fetches the latest release tag from the GitHub API (api.github.com/repos/agentcarousel/agentcarousel/releases/latest)
- Compares it against the running binary's version (env!('CARGO_PKG_VERSION'))
- If already up to date, prints a message and exits 0
- If an update is available, downloads the appropriate platform tarball from the release assets, verifies the SHA256 checksum against SHA256SUMS, replaces the running binary in-place (atomic rename), and prints the version it updated to
- Supports --check flag: prints whether an update is available without installing it (useful for CI/scripts)
- Respects a --yes / -y flag to skip the confirmation prompt in non-interactive shells

Platform detection should reuse the same target triple logic used in the install script (packaging/install.sh). The subcommand should live in crates/agentcarousel/src/cli/update.rs, following the pattern of existing subcommands.
close_reason: done

title: bug: trust_check temp pubkey file uses predictable PID-based name
description: resolve_pubkey_path() writes a fetched minisign public key to a temp file named agentcarousel-minisign-<PID>.pub. Because PIDs are small integers and publicly observable, an attacker on the same machine can pre-create that path before the write, causing minisign to verify against a malicious key. The update command already uses ULID for this (0.5.0 fix). Acceptance: use Ulid::new() in the format string, matching the pattern in cli/update.rs.
close_reason: done

title: Remove --config and --run-id from subcommands that don't use them
description: agc update, agc completions, and agc init all show --config and --run-id in their --help output because those flags are marked global = true on the top-level Cli struct. None of these three subcommands consume those flags: run_update(), run_completions(), and run_init() don't receive globals at all. The fix is to remove global = true from --config and --run-id on Cli, and instead add them explicitly to only the subcommand structs that actually read them (ValidateArgs, TestArgs, EvalArgs, ReportArgs, BundleArgs, PublishArgs, ExportArgs, TrustCheckArgs). The --no-color, --quiet, and --verbose flags can remain global since they affect output universally.
close_reason: done

title: bug: update.rs uses std::process::id() for temp file naming instead of Ulid
description: crates/agentcarousel/src/cli/update.rs:184 constructs the update temp file path as format!('.agentcarousel-update.{}', std::process::id()). The rest of the codebase uses Ulid::new() for temp paths (e.g. bundle.rs uses format!('agentcarousel-bundle-{}', Ulid::new())). std::process::id() is predictable and non-unique across process restarts; if two concurrent update runs happen (unlikely but possible), they would collide on the same temp path. Replace with Ulid::new() for consistency and collision safety.
close_reason: done

title: fix: strip=true silently does nothing with thin LTO on macOS
description: When strip=true is set in the release profile alongside lto='thin', the macOS strip pass leaves 33,802 symbols and ~2.6 MB of string table in the binary (confirmed via otool -l). With lto='fat' the same strip=true brings symbols down to 202. Root cause: thin LTO keeps cross-crate symbol references alive that strip cannot safely remove; fat LTO's global dead-code pass eliminates them first. We switched to lto='fat' in PR #XX to work around this, but the interaction should be documented and a CI size-regression check should be added so we notice if a future change regresses back to bloated thin-LTO builds.
close_reason: Fixed: switched lto = thin to lto = fat in release profile; strip=true now works correctly on macOS

title: E1: Implement BoundedCache with VecDeque FIFO eviction in runner/mod.rs
description: Add private struct BoundedCache { map: HashMap<String,EvalScores>, order: VecDeque<String>, capacity: usize } to runner/mod.rs. Methods: new(capacity), get(&str)->Option<&EvalScores>, insert(String,EvalScores). insert() skips if key exists; if len>=capacity pops front from order and removes from map; then pushes key to order and inserts into map.

title: feat: add color and styled help text across all CLI commands
description:
The current CLI help output is plain monochrome text. For a 1.0 product aimed at developers, the help experience should be polished: colored section headers, highlighted flag names, styled descriptions, and visual hierarchy that makes the CLI easy to scan and learn.

Work to do:
- Enable clap's built-in color support (clap feature 'color' and/or 'wrap_help') and confirm it is not being suppressed
- Use clap's styling API (clap::builder::styling) to apply a consistent color scheme: bold/colored command names and section headers (Commands, Options, Arguments), colored flag names (--flag), dimmed/muted description text where appropriate
- Respect NO_COLOR and TERM=dumb conventions (clap does this automatically; verify it is wired up)
- Review every subcommand's help strings for clarity and completeness — short descriptions should be one crisp line that tells a developer exactly what the command does; long help (shown with --help vs -h if we split them) can carry examples
- Add visible examples to the highest-traffic subcommands (validate, test, eval, bundle, trust-check) either via after_help or custom Help templates
- The overall aesthetic should be consistent with well-regarded Rust CLIs (e.g. cargo, rg, fd) — clean, scannable, no visual noise
close_reason: done

title: bug: process evaluator accepts any relative command from fixture YAML without an allowlist
description: crates/agentcarousel/src/evaluators/process.rs:60 runs Command::new(&self.command[0]) where command comes from a fixture's evaluator_config.process_cmd field. The constructor validates that the executable is not absolute and contains no '..' components, but any relative path that resolves via PATH is accepted (e.g. 'node', 'python3', 'sh'). A fixture from a third-party bundle could specify an unexpected interpreter. Mitigation options: (1) document the intended threat model explicitly (user-controlled fixtures = trusted); (2) add a config-level allowlist of permitted executables; (3) emit a warning when process_cmd is used with an executable not in a known set. This is intentional by design but the risk is undocumented and no validation occurs at bundle-pull time.
close_reason: done

title: Fix misleading --offline true in top-level quick-start example
description: The after_help text in Cli shows: 'agc test fixtures/skills/customer-support.yaml --filter-tags smoke --offline true'. The --offline flag exists on agc test, but agc test already defaults to mock mode (its description is 'Run fixtures with mock generation (no API keys required)'). Passing --offline true is therefore redundant and teaches users a noisy habit. Replace the quick-start example with one that demonstrates a flag that isn't the default — e.g. --filter-tags alone without --offline, or a concurrency/format flag — so the example has actual teaching value.
close_reason: done

title: Group CLI subcommands into human-readable categories in help output
description:
The agc help output lists all 13 subcommands in a flat list with no grouping. As the CLI grows, this becomes hard to scan. Clap supports subcommand display categories via .display_order() and custom next_display_order groups, or more powerfully via help_heading on each subcommand. Proposed grouping:

**Fixture work** (core loop): validate, test, eval, lint, init
**Results**: report, export
**Bundles & registry**: bundle, publish, trust-check
**Tooling**: completions, update, doctor

This makes the help output scannable at a glance and matches how users mentally group their workflow.
close_reason: done

title: chore: audit regex perf features pulled in via jsonschema -> fancy-regex
description: cargo-bloat shows regex_automata at 345 KB and regex_syntax at 110 KB in the release binary. Both are pulled in transitively: jsonschema -> fancy-regex -> regex (with default 'perf' features enabled, including perf-dfa, perf-backtrack, perf-onepass). The perf features trade binary size for runtime speed, which is probably not a worthwhile tradeoff for a CLI tool that compiles regexes at startup rather than in a hot loop. Investigate: (1) whether jsonschema exposes a feature gate to disable regex perf features, (2) whether a lighter JSON-schema crate (e.g. boon) would reduce this footprint, (3) measure actual savings with fat LTO since cross-crate DCE may already prune unused DFA tables.
close_reason: Audited: fancy-regex perf features can't be disabled without replacing jsonschema (uses_default_features=true, Cargo unions them). Fat LTO (just switched from thin) already reduced regex_automata 345→315 KB and regex_syntax 110→99 KB (~9%). Our own regex dep has same constraint. Switching to boon is a separate decision.
